skill.name: tzst
skill.description: Guides safe use of the tzst command-line tool for creating, extracting, listing, testing, installing, and automating .tzst or .tar.zst archives.
skill.summary: Use tzst CLI workflows for safe Zstandard tar archive management.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns, a hardcoded URL, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance indicators. Review found the command findings are Markdown examples and operational guidance for a CLI archive tool, while the weak-crypto and reconnaissance findings are false positives. The skill still carries medium risk because it encourages real archive creation, extraction, installation, and overwrite-prone workflows.

medium_findings.title: Real CLI Execution Guidance Can Modify Files
medium_findings.description: The skill explicitly says to default to execution for real archive actions when paths are known, and it provides create, extract, test, and list workflows. This is legitimate for the skill, but commands can create archives, extract files, overwrite existing paths, or operate on user-selected filesystem locations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files directly instruct use of real tzst CLI commands and include copy-paste command examples. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the commands match the declared archive-management purpose.

medium_findings.title: Archive Extraction Requires Careful Trust and Conflict Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill includes extraction filters and conflict-resolution options, including fully_trusted and replace_all. It recommends safer defaults, but misuse could extract unsafe archive contents or replace existing files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented options are visible and affect extraction behavior. The skill also documents safer defaults, so the concern is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

low_findings.title: Network Installation Reference Is Disclosed and Expected
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the upstream GitHub releases page for users who want a standalone binary. This is a normal installation path, but users should verify release provenance before installing binaries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is plainly documented as an install source and matches the project source. No hidden network request, credential transfer, or exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Weak-Cryptography and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to match archive-format text or exit-code documentation, not cryptographic code. The system-reconnaissance alert points to an argument parsing exit code, not host inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata, archive terminology, or exit-code tables. I found no evidence of cryptographic algorithm use or system reconnaissance logic in the reviewed files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Command Examples for External CLI Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The detected backtick patterns are Markdown command examples for tzst, uv, and pip rather than Ruby shell execution. They still represent commands an assistant may run, so they should remain visible as external command risk evidence.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The context is Markdown documentation, not executable Ruby code. The commands are nevertheless specific external programs that could be executed during skill use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Binary Download Source
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill references a GitHub releases URL for standalone binary installation. This is not hidden networking, but it can lead users to download and execute third-party release artifacts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly presented as an installation option. I did not find evidence of automatic download or covert network behavior.

content.value_statement: Archive workflows can fail when flags, filters, or conflict handling are unclear. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through safer tzst CLI commands for creation, extraction, inspection, and automation.
actual_capabilities: Selects the right tzst subcommand for create, extract, flatten, list, and test tasks.
Provides install and preflight checks using tzst, uv, pip, or upstream release assets.
Recommends safer extraction filters and explains when fully trusted mode is appropriate.
Builds script-friendly commands with JSON output, no banner mode, and exit-code guidance.
Helps choose streaming, compression level, output directory, and conflict-resolution flags.
limitations: It covers the tzst CLI only, not the Python API or unrelated archive tools.
It cannot verify archive safety without inspecting the actual files and source.
It depends on the installed tzst version and local command availability.
It should not run destructive extraction or replacement commands without clear user intent.

use_cases.title: Create Release Archives
use_cases.description: Package project directories into .tzst or .tar.zst archives with suitable compression and atomic creation defaults.

use_cases.title: Restore Backups Carefully
use_cases.description: Extract archives with preserved paths, safe filters, streaming mode, and explicit conflict handling.

use_cases.title: Automate Archive Checks
use_cases.description: List or test archives with machine-readable output and predictable exit-code behavior for scripts.

prompt_templates.title: List Archive Contents
prompt_templates.prompt: Use tzst to list the contents of backup.tzst. Show the safest command and explain what it does.

prompt_templates.title: Extract With Safe Defaults
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract backup.tzst into restore/ while preserving paths. Use the safest normal extraction filter.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Compressed Archive
prompt_templates.prompt: Create project-backup.tzst from src/, docs/, and package files. Use a higher compression level and keep atomic creation enabled.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Automation Command
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a script-friendly tzst command to extract backup.tzst into restore/ with JSON output and a non-interactive conflict policy.

output_examples.input: List what is inside backup.tzst before extracting it.
output_examples.output: The assistant provides a tzst list command, explains that it inspects archive members, and suggests verbose output when details are needed.

output_examples.input: Extract a backup into restore without prompts in automation.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends preserving directory structure, using the data filter, enabling JSON-friendly output, and choosing an explicit conflict policy.

output_examples.input: Make a .tar.zst archive from project folders.
output_examples.output: The assistant chooses the create subcommand, keeps atomic creation enabled, and explains compression level selection.

best_practices: List archive contents before selective extraction when member names are uncertain.
Use the data filter by default and reserve fully trusted mode for verified sources.
Choose explicit conflict-resolution values for non-interactive or scripted extraction.
anti_patterns: Flattening an archive when the user expects the original directory structure.
Installing or running standalone binaries without checking the source and version.
Combining machine-readable output with interactive conflict prompts.

faq.question: What archive formats does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on tzst CLI workflows for .tzst and .tar.zst archives.

faq.question: Can it help install tzst?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers uv, pip, and upstream release binary options, with a preflight version check.

faq.question: Does it support the Python tzst API?
faq.answer: No. It is for CLI workflows and should defer Python API questions to general library documentation.

faq.question: Is fully trusted extraction recommended?
faq.answer: No. The skill says to use fully trusted mode only when the archive source is explicitly trusted.

faq.question: Can it produce commands for automation?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends JSON output, no banner mode, exit-code handling, and non-interactive conflict choices.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill may run real archive and install commands, which can modify files or install software if misused.
